speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. We are interviewing the survivor Mr. Milton S. Hirschfeld. Interviewer, Anita White. Mr. Hirschfeld, we were just speaking. You were just telling us about the [INAUDIBLE] contingent, which came in and was saved by-- those who were not dead were saved by the people in Brunnlitz. Now, what happens afterwards in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the camp was normal. And the camp was not bad, because anything that somebody who was at Schindler can tell you-- he'll tell you that he was in prison-- he was in, but the condition was all right. You could live. You could live through. And that's what it was with us. Schindler tried-- every weekend, he tried to go-- he was going out for the weekend. When he came back, he brought bread for everybody, one additional bread, which was a tremendous attitude. So we had enough to eat. Otherwise, the food, which they made there, was better also because it was not the same as in the-- in the other concentration camp, where you were really dependent on the one who gives you. Whether he gives you with the thick stuff or just water, and you had to survive the whole day. We worked in the factory. And some people say-- I don't know how much true is it-- that nothing that we did was right. But I don't believe in it, because we made it to measures and everything to what it was supposed to be. But they say that he had to buy sometimes some missiles, some shells, and to give it to tell them that this is it. I don't believe in this because I know that we did. And there was checking, and the people who worked there on these presses-- they were-- they were normal people. They were not people like in another camp where they were depressed and [? all ?] [? that-- ?] people who-- and we had a radio. There was a radioman there who repaired all the radios and all the telephones. So we had already-- we knew what's going out around the world, and we knew that we will be-- that we have another half a year, only, to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the guards in that Brunnlitz camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards were Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS. Yes, they were SS. Later on came others. They came. We had another. You know, once we got these from [? Goulesev ?] people. And the rest, of course, the ones were not alive, they made a Jewish funeral for them. Then we got some other people from other camps. There came a couple from another camp who was a murderer, and they sent him to us. And we were suspicious of him. Despite the fact that he didn't do anything to us, we were suspicious, and we didn't want to do too much with him. As a matter of fact, at the end of the-- when we knew already that we were liberated, they hung him, because there were some people who knew him from before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- now, did the camp have a feeling, an idea, that the war was coming to an end? And were you apprehensive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew about it. We knew about it. And toward the end, Schindler was afraid that the Germans will kill us like they did with other camps. And apparently, when the order came in, the messenger was thrown out from the steps. And the message never came into [? Lippold ?]. What I can say from my experience, the fact in film, which they show in film, is not the true fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which fact?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact at the end, that the soldiers are sitting-- standing, and they them to do whatever they want. This is not true. The soldiers disappeared two, three nights before the end of the war. They disappeared. We didn't know when and what. There was no guard. [? Lippold ?] disappeared. Everything disappeared. We didn't have anybody. We waited the two days until a Russian-- couple of Russian soldiers came in, and they told us that we are free, that we'll have-- from now, we will have as much potatoes as we want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Schindler-- was Schindler-- was his family still there in the camp when the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date when the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler escaped with the Jews because they gave him a uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] uniform, so that he looks like a Jew. And he escaped from-- Who of his family-- There were two or three Jewish people who went with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. His wife, no. Only him. His wife could go there. She left. She could live there. They have a house there from before they knew her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took him. They wanted to bring him to the American border. And then he was sent to America. Now, what happened when the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians came in-- they didn't come in. There were no soldiers. Nobody saw soldiers. These soldiers, they were very poorly equipped. The only thing that they had a lot were watches. They had five watches on each hand. But otherwise, they didn't have ammunition, they didn't have-- they wear on their-- the guns didn't have leather, they had strings-- chords put together. These were older people. These were not the front line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happens to the camp and to you and the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp were lose. Everybody could do-- the Czechs came in and they brought us something to eat in the beginning. And they told us we can go into the private houses in which Germans, formerly, they lived. And we were there. We lived there for three days, I think, for four days. And then everybody did on his own whatever he wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So some people wanted to go to Krakow best, and they started-- and we wanted-- we decided, our whole group, that we wanted to go to-- we want to go to America. But we didn't-- we didn't want to say that we're going to America because [INAUDIBLE] So we said that we wanted to go to Paris, and from Paris, we'll go to Israel. The best way to go to Paris was to go to the-- to Prague because from Prague, there was already a train running through Germany. So the Russians were already everywhere. They made contact with the Americans already, so there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Prague, and we-- somehow we-- I don't even remember whether we paid or we didn't pay, because we said that we are going back to Paris. So we were like people who are repatriated. So we went on the train with soldiers, with French soldiers who were going to Paris. And with this train, we came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped in one place. We stopped in Germany. We had to be there. We had to get some documents. So we were there for about three days or two days, or three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name of this camp. There was a DP camp there. It was not a concentration camp, it was a DP camp. And from there, we went to Paris through Mulhouse. Mulhouse was the border town where they gave us, again, a bath and everything. They give us something put on. And we went from there. We went to Paris. Once we came to Paris, we told them that we are not-- we told them already on the border. We got to Mulhouse. We got the carte de rapatrie, which means-- and they wrote on this card that we are born in Poland and we are going only to Paris. When we came to Paris, they directed us to a Jewish community organization which was supported by joint distribution committee. And we got from them everything. First of all, they gave us-- the first day when we came in, they told us where we'll sleep. There was a hotel, Hotel Lutetia, which was one of the nicest hotels, which was occupied during the war by the Gestapo. They have all the offices there. Everybody from the Gestapo was there. And they told us-- even somebody of the-- of the porters told us that a few days, ago or a week before, there came a letter from the Vichy government to the Gestapo, addressed to the Gestapo, for payment for the deportation of Jews in the last few days, which they got orders. And as a matter of fact, I read it this year, somewhere in a paper, about it. That's how meticulous the Frenchman were, and what kind of-- how exactly they were, and which train they put the people, and who were the people with all the names, and which train they went. And the train never came in and never went because there were shortness-- shortness of trains. So they didn't let them through anywhere. And anyway, they came already. The Americans were already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there in Lutetia for a few days. And we went to Jewish community [? there, ?] and they gave us whatever we needed. They gave us some money. Gave us-- they gave us alimentary card. We got double alimentary card in French.

speaker: interviewee
text: Looking back, the life in France for the four years which we were there is one of the nicest parts that I remember from the past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- were they-- all those four years, were you helped by the JDC, or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. In the beginning, they helped us. They sent us for vacations twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to the south of France, to [? La ?] [? Bussiere ?], which was near, about south of Lyons, and near Valence. And we were there for a month or even more than a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that for survivors only?

speaker: interviewee
text: For survivors only, it was, yes. Camp de deporte, yes. And there were a lot of people there. And some of these came later to America. We received-- we applied for affidavits to come to America, and we got them very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean very--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we registered in the-- we registered in Paris in the American consulate very early, so we got numbers 2, 3, 4, or something like this-- just the beginning numbers. However, when we came to pick up the visas which were ready, all of a sudden, on a Monday morning, there was a sign that all the visas are kept back because priority have the DP camps in Germany. So that's why we stayed there for four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: | started to take some courses-- not at the university, but some chemical courses concerning cosmetology. This was for the evening. And then in the morning during the day, the last two years, I worked for a house for children which came from Poland, which is a very known fact. It was even a small film made. It was Without parents, just which were found in Poland, where they survived. And this was the house where they prepared them. They taught them and they prepared them to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: The organization was [INAUDIBLE], which is [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE]. [FRENCH] L'Oeuvre de Protection des Enfants Juifs. And it belonged-- of course, there were supported by Jewish [INAUDIBLE]. And from time to time, there came from America, big, rich Jews who gave donation for this. I remember one of them. I remember the name. It was Mr. Zale, the Zale-- the Zale who have Z Zale Jewelry. It was a big outfit, Zale Jewelry Company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zale, Z-A-L-E. Zale. And they came in and they supported-- they sent in from America. Packages came in all the time with clothing. And the children waited and waited until finally, they went to Israel. Even one time-- we send them from-- I was the [INAUDIBLE] in this house, and they sent them from there to-- this was not in Paris itself. It was in [? Bellevue. ?] This was a suburb of Paris. And from there, they sent them to Marseille. From Marseille, they were put on a boat. And I think that the first boat that they put them on was even an illegal boat. But the others were already illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your wife and your sister do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was working because she's a professional in suits-- in making suits. And my wife worked as an assistant in a dental office in the place where we lived, because we didn't have all the time at Lutetia. This was a hotel. But they put us over in a home which was for students.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did finally your papers come through?

speaker: interviewee
text: The paper came through-- the papers came through in June-- in June, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said-- didn't you-- did you stay in Paris for four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I'm sorry. I'm sorry. 1949. June, 1949. Because we were four years. And the papers came in, and we didn't wait too long. We took the-- took the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came into the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the first day we were in a hotel, on Broadway. And then we rented an apartment. Of course, we went also to the HIAS, and they gave us support in the beginning, as much as we needed. And my wife became pregnant almost-- my son was born in March. So in March, 1950. So she worked for a time-- for a short time, she worked at Helena Rubinstein, in the place, for a few months. And I got a job, a steady job, already in December. And then we moved already from the temporary apartment that we had on Riverside Drive. We moved to Queens where we got an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job, that you said? You got a permanent job.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got  job, permanent job with a company importing watches from Switzerland and distributing in the United States. And I started working there in the service department, and then I developed. I became Vice President and I worked all my life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I retired last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you retired last year. Now, how many children do have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two children. Michael is the son and Audrey is the daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they both married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are both married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have grandchildren. I have two grand-- my son has a boy and a girl, and my daughter has two girls, one just born a month ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Congratulations!

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to thank you, Mr. Hirschfeld, for a very interesting and very moving testimony. And I thank you for sharing your experiences with us. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. This is the registration book as a student at the University of Krakow, which they call the University Jagiellonski. It's written in Latin like everything was done at that time. On the left is my picture from that time, which means from 1937, and the stamp of the university. This is a picture taken on the roof of the-- of the Jewish community in Krakow. And I am on the left-- I am on the left side sitting, the first one from the left side setting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1939 in November, 1939, in the beginning, after the Germans came in. And I was working at that time in the resettlement bureau, which gave out identification to all Jewish people. This is a picture made in 1946 in [? La ?] [? Bussiere ?] in the south of France right after-- where we were on vacation, sent by the Jewish community in Paris. And the house, it was a maison de deporte. It was only for the people who came back from the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the picture in the second row-- in the first-- in the middle row standing, the fourth from the right. And my wife is sitting, the third one from the right. This is a picture also taken in [? La ?] [? Bussiere ?], on vacation in France. It was in 1946. It is myself and my wife here. This is a picture of me and my sister taken in 1948 in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister [? Polda. ?] This picture was taken on the when we arrived to the United States in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am here. I'm the first one with the hands up on the left side-- on the right side. This is my picture with John Keneally, the author of the book when I worked for him, giving him the information for this book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what you did working with him. Were you interviewed by him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were sitting and we were telling him the stories. He didn't know anything. So he asked us questions. And this was the first day in New York, or the second day when he came in from Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people from the were with him then? You? Two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Just me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a private home. In my private home. This is my daughter, Audrey, with her-- with my granddaughter, Jessica, taken in her apartment this year. This is my son with his wife-- Michael, with his wife, [? Gray ?], and son, Edmund, and daughter, Natasha. This was taken in Martha's Vineyard about 1991. This is the carte de rapatrie, which we received when we entered the French territory right after the war, in 1945. As you see, there is a date, June 16, 1945. That's the date when we crossed the French border from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the city you got it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the city was Mulhouse. And it was on the border at that time. This is the award, which I received just recently, on October the 22nd this year. And from our organization, the New Krakow Friendship Society, which is an organization of survivors, and which takes part in supporting people who are indigent or who need some help, and sending all help to Israel, whatever needed. And I served from the inception with this first as a board of-- on the board of directors and then on the executive board as a treasurer, as a vice president, and as a president recently. This is the cup which I received when I-- when I-- as long as-- after I finished my term of the president of the New Krakow Friendship Society, which was also this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hirschfeld, would you please introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The first one from the left is Natasha. This is daughter of Michael. And she will be 5 pretty soon. And next to her is her brother, Ned, which is short from Edmund. And he will be 9 pretty soon. MICHAEL (SON): No, he is 9. AUDREY (DAUGHTER): He is 9. He is 9. Oh, he is 9, yes. He is 9. And next to them is their father, Michael. And next to him is Jessica, who will be pretty soon 4. And she is the daughter of Audrey, who sits near her, which is her mother. And I'm next to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. AUDREY (DAUGHTER): And we forgot your baby sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the babysitter they know, because I told them. Baby sister, yes. She's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Jessica--

speaker: interviewee
text: She's just over 1 month old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jessica, would you like to tell us now when your next birthday comes? And tell us about your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): OK. On my birthday, I'm going to be 4. And soon I'll be in kid aerobics. And my baby sister was-- need to change her diaper. And I was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: AUDREY (DAUGHTER): You were holding her?

speaker: interviewee
text: JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): And [INAUDIBLE] was burping the baby and feeding the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): And she was changing the baby. Oh, that's very interesting. And what is the name of your baby sister? JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Mallory. Mallory. Oh, are you mommy's helper? JESSICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Mm-hm. Yes. And Natasha, could you tell us-- could you tell us when your next birthday is going to be, and how old will you be? NATASHA (GRANDDAUGHTER): My birthday's going to be in January. And how old will you be then? NATASHA (GRANDDAUGHTER): In New Years, and I'll be 5. 5. Do you want to tell us something about your grandpa? What do you like about him most? NATASHA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Well, he always makes dinner so good [INAUDIBLE]. And you enjoy his cooking. And you know that he loves you a great deal? And do you love him? Ned, would you tell us a little bit about your schooling? Now, what grade are you in? NED (GRANDSON): Fourth. Fourth grade. And do you want to say something to your grandpa now that-- you're-- you're an older young man already. You're a grown-up almost. What would you like to say about your grandpa? NED (GRANDSON): Well, I have a lot of fun whenever I come to his house, but it's not often. And usually, we have a barbecue dinner. And I like that a lot. And I like kicking the soccer ball around a little in his backyard. So he is like your friend. Almost like a friend. NED (GRANDSON): Yeah. Yes. Thank you. MICHAEL (SON): And you can beat him at chess. NED (GRANDSON): And I can beat him at chess. Oh, you play chess? NED (GRANDSON): Yeah. You play chess a long time already? NED (GRANDSON): Yeah. Oh, well, that's marvelous. That's a very good game. Audrey, what-- when you were growing up, did you feel that your upbringing-- because your father went through such very difficult experiences-- that yours and Michael's upbringing was different, and gave you a different point of view of the world and the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: AUDREY (DAUGHTER): Actually, we were just talking about that. And we decided that it really didn't because our parents never spoke about the war, about being in the concentration camps. In fact, it was sort of a forbidden subject. I remember once when someone asked my mother about her blue tattoo, and what was that, you know, there was a hush over the room and it was not something you spoke about much. So I'm sure our upbringing was different, but we weren't aware of it at the time. And all of our cousins and the people we socialized with, their parents were survivors, as well. So for us, this was the norm. And it really wasn't until, you know, really, the last 10 years, or I guess since was written, that it was something that was even spoken about in our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you father's and mother's experiences were not discussed with you as you were growing up. AUDREY (DAUGHTER): Not at all. So it gave you a chance to grow up as any other American young person grew up, or-- [LAUGHS] Or are you-- well? AUDREY (DAUGHTER): I'm not sure. Michael, what would you like to add to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: MICHAEL (SON): I think it's very accurate. I remember-- because I'm a few years older-- sometimes it seems like quite a few years older. I can remember my parents getting together with their friends, who were likewise survivors, in the evening, and Polish was the language that was spoken at home. And I knew Polish then better than I do today. And I used to be able to stay up at night when they thought I was in bed, and listen to the conversations. And everybody-- I remember, everybody used to speak at the same time. It was a really tremendous noise that they made whenever they got together. But I remember during these evening get-togethers, there were any number of words that I didn't understand, and had not heard during the course of the day. And it wasn't until the publication of that I saw those words in a-- in a format where they suddenly had meaning. All the proper names, the name of the factory, the Emalia, the name of the concentration camp commandant. These were words that I'd heard and hadn't understood before. So they really did protect us from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hirschfield, do you have a message, in a few words, to your grandchildren? What do you take out from your experience about the world and people? What would you like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just have to know that something like this can happen, and they should know what it was. And they should be on the-- on the-- they should be watchful for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you think it may happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything can happen again. Nobody believed in '39 or in '38 that something like this would happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
